content:
Less prompting, better automation.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Solen Feyissa on UnsplashA few months ago, I opened a folder on my laptop that contained nothing but prompt experiments. At first, there were only ten files. Then twenty. Before I realized it, I had tested more than one hundred Claude prompts while building Python scripts, automating repetitive work, writing technical documentation, and experimenting with AI workflows.
Most of them were disappointing.
Some produced beautiful-looking answers that completely fell apart the moment I tried to automate them. Others worked once and never again. A few were simply too verbose to be useful inside real projects.
But hidden among those hundred prompts were a handful that completely changed how I build software.
Not because they magically wrote perfect code.
Because they dramatically reduced the amount of thinking I had to do before writing code.
That’s an important difference.
One lesson I’ve learned after four years of working with Python is that developers rarely lose time writing syntax. They lose time deciding what to build next, debugging unclear requirements, or translating ideas into implementation plans.
